title: Full AI Stack — Agent Potato: License to Chat

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you will use agent mode to complete a mission: build a complete feature across a full-stack codebase using only Copilot Agent Mode.

We've cloned the OctoCAT Supply project for you. Follow the steps in this panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Read the Mission Briefing
steps.instruction:
Read the mission briefing on the slide. This is your challenge for today.

You need to deliver three things using only agent mode:
• A new API endpoint
• A React component that consumes it
• Passing tests

No manual file browsing. No copy-pasting from docs. Agent mode is your only tool.

Keep the success criteria in mind as you work through each section.

steps.title: Explore the Project Structure
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer panel and take a quick look at the project's top-level folders. You should see frontend/, api/, a Makefile, and a docker-compose.yml.

Don't dive into the files. Close the Explorer when you've oriented yourself. Agent mode will handle the deep exploration.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E

steps.title: Start Your Reconnaissance
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and select Agent mode from the dropdown.

Your first task: use agent mode to build a mental model of this codebase. Ask it to explain the project structure, tech stack, API routes, database schema, and frontend pages.

Start broad, then drill down. The slide shows the kind of questions that work well.
steps.tip: Good exploration prompts start broad ("What does this project do?") and narrow down ("What routes exist?", "What database tables are defined?").

steps.title: Build Your Mental Model
steps.instruction:
Keep exploring until you can answer these without opening a file:

• What frameworks does the frontend use?
• How are API routes organized?
• What database is used and how is it accessed?
• What testing framework is in place?

This knowledge will be essential for writing effective prompts later.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Review Approval Gates
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat in agent mode and ask it to run the project's test suite.

Watch for the approval prompt before the terminal command executes. Review the command and click Continue to approve it.
steps.tip: The command to run tests in this project is make test. Try asking agent mode to discover and run it.

steps.title: Check Auto-Approve Settings
steps.instruction:
Open Settings and search for github.copilot.chat.agent.autoApprove. This controls whether agent mode runs terminal commands without asking.

Leave it off for now. Manual approval teaches you what commands the agent wants to run.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel and settings tab for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Settings: Ctrl+,

steps.title: Discover Mode Commands
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and type Chat: Change Mode to see the available commands for switching between Ask, Edit, and Agent modes.

Close the palette when you've seen the options.

steps.title: Try Ask Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch to Ask mode using the mode dropdown. Ask a question about the codebase to see how it differs from agent mode.

Notice that Ask mode gives you an answer but does not create or modify any files.
steps.tip: Try asking about the testing framework or the frontend routing. Compare the depth of response to what agent mode provided.

steps.title: Try Edit Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch to Edit mode. Open a source file from the API routes and ask Copilot to add documentation to it.

Edit mode proposes diffs that you accept or reject individually. It is precise and targeted.

steps.title: Back to Agent Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch back to Agent mode for the rest of the lab. You now know when to reach for each mode:

• Ask for exploration and questions
• Edit for targeted single-file changes
• Agent for multi-step, multi-file builds

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create Your Instructions File
steps.instruction:
Before building anything, set up a .github/copilot-instructions.md file for this project.

Think about what you learned during reconnaissance: the frameworks, testing tools, database patterns, and style conventions. Encode those as rules so every Copilot response follows the project's conventions.

The slide shows an example of what good instructions look like.
steps.tip: Key rules to include: TypeScript strict mode, React 18 with hooks, Vitest (not Jest), Tailwind CSS, parameterized SQLite queries, named exports, async/await.

steps.title: Save and Verify
steps.instruction:
Save the file as .github/copilot-instructions.md. Then open Copilot Chat in agent mode and ask it what instructions it has loaded for this workspace.

Copilot should reference your new file. From here on, every response will follow your project's conventions.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create a Scoped Instruction File
steps.instruction:
Create a scoped instruction file that applies only to React component files. Save it at .github/instructions/react-components.instructions.md.

Think about what conventions should apply specifically to frontend TSX files: component patterns, data fetching approach, styling framework, and TypeScript type requirements.
steps.tip: Use applyTo: "frontend/src/**/*.tsx" in the YAML frontmatter. Include rules about functional components, React Query, Tailwind utilities, and typed props.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Build the Backend: Your Mission Starts Now
steps.instruction:
This is where the mission begins. Use agent mode to create the API endpoint described in the mission briefing.

Write a prompt that tells agent mode exactly what endpoint to create, which existing route to follow as a pattern, and that it should include tests. Remember the prompting techniques from the slide: state the goal, specify constraints, define done, reference existing code.
steps.tip: Point agent mode at an existing route file for convention reference. Ask it to register the route and run the tests. The slides cover what makes a good agent prompt.

steps.title: Review and Iterate
steps.instruction:
Review the diffs that agent mode produced. Check that:

• The route follows existing conventions
• Database queries use parameterized statements
• Tests use the correct framework
• The route is registered in the API entry point

If something looks wrong, tell agent mode what to fix. Let it iterate.

steps.title: Verify Tests Pass
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and run the test suite yourself to confirm the tests pass independently of agent mode.

Verifying outside the agent's loop builds confidence that the code genuinely works.
steps.tip: Run make test in the terminal. If tests fail, go back to agent mode and describe the failure.

steps.title: Review Changes in Source Control
steps.instruction:
Open the Source Control panel to see every file agent mode created or modified. Review the diffs.

Look for files you didn't expect. Agent mode sometimes creates helper files or modifies configuration beyond what you asked for.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G

steps.title: Build the Frontend Component
steps.instruction:
Now tackle the second deliverable. Use agent mode to create a React component that fetches data from your new endpoint and displays it.

Think about what your prompt needs to include: the component file path, which API endpoint to call, an existing component to use as a pattern reference, and that it should add routing and navigation.
steps.tip: Reference an existing page component so agent mode extracts the data fetching and styling patterns. Ask it to add a route and a sidebar navigation link.

steps.title: Verify the Frontend
steps.instruction:
Ask agent mode to start the development servers so you can verify the feature works end-to-end.

Check that the API serves data, the frontend page renders, and the navigation link appears in the sidebar.
steps.tip: If the component doesn't render correctly, describe the problem to agent mode. For example: "The table is empty even though the API returns data."

validate.label: Component fetches from the inventory endpoint

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Strengthen Your Tests
steps.instruction:
Use agent mode to improve test coverage for your feature. Think about the cases the slides describe: the happy path, the empty result case, and error handling.

Write a prompt that specifies which scenarios to test and which existing test files to follow as patterns.
steps.tip: Good test prompts specify scenarios: "Test the happy path with valid data, the empty result case, and the case where the database query fails."

steps.title: Run the Full Test Suite
steps.instruction: Ask agent mode to run the complete test suite and fix any failures. This verifies your new code doesn't break existing functionality.

steps.title: Verify Tests Independently
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and run the test suite yourself. Compare the output to what agent mode reported.

This catches cases where the agent's test run succeeded in a modified environment but fails in a clean run.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Iterative Refinement
steps.instruction:
Review the code agent mode generated and provide targeted feedback. Think about error handling, input validation, and API documentation.

Try using the right mode for each fix: agent mode for multi-file changes, edit mode for surgical fixes, ask mode for design questions.
steps.tip: Try switching to Edit mode for a small fix like adding Swagger documentation to a single endpoint. The precision of Edit mode is better for single-file changes.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Cross-Stack Rename Challenge
steps.instruction:
Test your agent mode skills with a cross-stack refactor. Pick a field name in your inventory feature and rename it across the entire stack: API response, TypeScript types, frontend component, and test assertions.

Write a single prompt that describes the rename and asks agent mode to verify nothing broke afterward.
steps.tip: A well-scoped rename prompt includes: the old name, the new name, which files to check, and a test run at the end.

steps.title: Verify the Rename
steps.instruction:
Open Source Control to review all changed files. The diff view shows exactly what was renamed and whether any references were missed.

This is a habit worth building: agent mode proposes, Source Control verifies.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G

steps.title: Create a Reusable Prompt File
steps.instruction:
Save one of your best agent mode workflows as a reusable prompt file. Create a .github/prompts/ file that captures a common task for this project.

Use input variables so the prompt can be reused with different parameters. The slide shows the syntax and an example.
steps.tip: A good prompt file for this project might scaffold a new API endpoint: route file, test file, route registration, and a test run. Use ${input:routePath} for variable parts.

steps.title: Run the Prompt File
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and type / to open the prompt picker. Select your new prompt file and give it a test run with a different route name.

If the prompt produces good output, your whole team benefits from your work.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Compare Your Approach
steps.instruction:
Review the reference solution on the slide. Compare it to what you did:

• Did you follow the same phasing (reconnaissance, instructions, backend, frontend, testing)?
• Did your prompts reference existing code?
• How many iterations did agent mode need?

There is no single correct path, but the reference represents a proven workflow.

steps.title: Final Mission Validation
steps.instruction:
Time to verify your mission results. Click Check Mission Progress below to validate that all three deliverables are in place:

• The inventory API endpoint exists
• The React component exists and fetches data
• All tests pass

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Mission accomplished! You've completed Agent Potato: License to Chat.

You now know how to:
• Use agent mode to navigate and build across a full-stack codebase
• Write effective prompts with references to existing code
• Set up instructions that shape every Copilot response
• Switch between Ask, Edit, and Agent modes for the right task
• Create reusable prompt files for your team

Next courses: MCP Maximizer to extend agent mode's tools, or Coding Agent: It's Alive! for cloud-based autonomous agents.
